title: Wildfire Prevention Act of 2025
stage_label: Committee on Energy and Natural Resources Subcommittee on Public Lands, Forests, and Mining. Hearings held.
plain_summary: This bill requires federal agencies to do much more forest management to prevent wildfires, including cutting down trees and doing controlled burns. The agencies must increase these activities by at least forty percent by 2029 compared to recent years. The bill also makes it easier and faster to get approval for removing dangerous trees and clearing vegetation around power lines. It creates a program to test new wildfire prevention technologies and gives local governments and tribes the right to join court cases about forest management projects.
last_action_text: Committee on Energy and Natural Resources Subcommittee on Public Lands, Forests, and Mining. Hearings held.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title; Table of Contents
sections.text: This law can be called the Wildfire Prevention Act of twenty twenty five. This section also shows the table of contents which lists all the parts of this law. The law has three main titles. Title one is called Accomplishments Over Rhetoric and covers how to speed up forest treatments, create reports, and track forest work. Title two is called Forest Management and covers managing plants around power lines, timber sales, removing dangerous trees, who can join lawsuits, and using grazing animals to reduce fire risk. Title three is called Cultural Change in Agencies and covers requiring agencies to use existing tools, creating partnerships to test new wildfire technology, and getting rid of certain reports.

sections.title: Section 2  Definitions
sections.text: This section explains what important terms mean in this law. Federal land means land in the National Forest System and public lands managed by the Bureau of Land Management. Hazardous fuels reduction activity means any work to manage plants and reduce wildfire risk, like cutting trees or doing controlled burns, but does not include signing contracts for this work. National Forest System means the system of forests managed by the Forest Service, but does not include forest reserves not created from public domain land. Secretary concerned means either the Secretary of Agriculture working through the Forest Service Chief for National Forest land, or the Secretary of the Interior working through the Bureau of Land Management Director for other public lands. Wildland-urban interface means areas where homes and wildlands meet, as defined in an existing law from two thousand three.

sections.title: Section 101  Accelerating Treatments on Federal Land
sections.text: The government agencies must figure out their baseline numbers for forest treatments. They must look at how many acres they thinned trees and did controlled burns each year from twenty nineteen through twenty twenty three, then calculate the average. Starting in twenty twenty five, agencies must set yearly goals for tree thinning and controlled burns. For twenty twenty five and twenty twenty six, the goals must be at least as high as the baseline average. For twenty twenty seven and twenty twenty eight, goals must be twenty percent higher than the baseline. Starting in twenty twenty nine, goals must be forty percent higher than the baseline. Within ninety days of this law passing, and every year after, agencies must assign how many acres each region or state will work on. All this information must be made public on agency websites. This section does not override other laws like the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act or the Wilderness Act. Setting these goals and assignments does not require environmental reviews under NEPA (the National Environmental Policy Act).

sections.title: Section 102  Annual Reports
sections.text: Starting by September thirtieth twenty twenty five and every year after, the Forest Service and Bureau of Land Management must publish reports on their websites about the previous year. The reports must include how many acres were treated under the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, how many acres had trees thinned and controlled burns, and whether they met their yearly goals. They must explain any problems that stopped them from meeting goals, like lawsuits or delays in environmental paperwork. The reports must show how many acres had regeneration harvests (cutting trees to help new ones grow). They must identify which treated acres are in areas where at least twenty five percent of trees might die from bugs and diseases over fifteen years, or areas with very high or high wildfire danger. The reports must show how many acres used streamlined environmental review processes like categorical exclusions or emergency authorities. They must also show how many acres involved partnerships with states, tribes, or private groups through various agreement programs.

sections.title: Section 103  Transparency in Hazardous Fuels Reduction Activity Reporting
sections.text: Agencies must include detailed reports about hazardous fuels reduction work in their budget materials sent to the President. From twenty twenty five through twenty thirty, they must report using both their old counting methods and new methods described in this section. Starting in twenty thirty one, they must only use the new methods. The new methods require agencies to count each acre only once per year, even if multiple treatments happened on that acre. Reports must show which acres are in wildland-urban interface areas, the wildfire risk level at the start and end of each year, what types of treatments were done, the cost per acre, which region the work was in, and how effective the treatments were at reducing wildfire risk. All reports must be made public on agency websites. Within ninety days of this law passing, agencies must create standard procedures for tracking this data. The procedures must include regular data reviews, verification methods, analysis of treatment effectiveness, and ways to distinguish between acres inside and outside the wildland-urban interface. Within fourteen days of implementing these procedures, agencies must send Congress a report describing the procedures and recommending any policy changes needed.

sections.title: Section 104  Regional Forest Carbon Accounting
sections.text: Starting by September thirtieth twenty twenty five and every three years after, the Forest Service must figure out the net forest carbon balance for each Forest Service region. This means determining whether National Forest land in each region is a carbon source (releases more carbon than it stores) or a carbon sink (stores more carbon than it releases). The Forest Service must use data from their forest inventory and analysis program to make these calculations. They must publish this information on the Forest Service website.

sections.title: Section 105  Wildland Fire Performance Metrics
sections.text: Within eighteen months of this law passing, the agencies must send a report to four Congressional committees about how they measure wildfire risk reduction performance. The report must identify solutions for tracking hazardous fuels reduction work and forest restoration treatments. It must include strategies to track whether land management reduces wildfire dangers, track local outcomes, standardize national monitoring, quantify catastrophic wildfire risk reduction, identify problems preventing annual wildfire risk mapping updates, and use advanced technologies to improve information quality. The report goes to the Senate Committee on Energy and Natural Resources, Senate Committee on Agriculture Nutrition and Forestry, House Committee on Natural Resources, and House Committee on Agriculture.

sections.title: Section 201  Vegetation Management, Facility Inspection, and Operation and Maintenance Relating to Electric Transmission and Distribution Facility Rights-of-Way
sections.text: This section changes existing law about managing plants around electric power lines on federal land. It increases the distance from ten feet to fifty feet around power lines where dangerous trees can be removed without extra permits. It also allows electric utility companies to cut and remove trees or other plants near their power lines on National Forest or Bureau of Land Management land without needing separate timber sales, as long as the work follows applicable plans and environmental laws. If the utility company sells any of the removed material, they must give the government any money they get from the sale, minus transportation costs. However, they are not required to sell the removed material.

sections.title: Section 202  Timber Sales on National Forest System Land
sections.text: This section increases the dollar limit for certain timber sales on National Forest land from ten thousand dollars to fifty five thousand dollars. This change affects when special procedures are required for timber sales under the National Forest Management Act.

sections.title: Section 203  Categorical Exclusion for High-Priority Hazard Trees
sections.text: This section defines high-priority hazard trees as standing trees that present visible dangers to people or federal property due to damage or deterioration, are very likely to fall and cause injury or damage, and are within three hundred feet of certain National Forest roads, along forest trails, or in developed recreation sites. High-priority hazard tree activities include pruning, cutting down, and disposing of these dangerous trees, but exclude work in wilderness areas, building permanent roads or trails, work on land where Congress or the President has restricted tree removal, work that conflicts with forest plans, or work in roadless areas. Within one year of this law passing, the Secretary of Agriculture must create a categorical exclusion (a type of streamlined environmental review) for high-priority hazard tree activities. Projects using this categorical exclusion cannot exceed three thousand acres. The Secretary must still follow environmental laws and apply extraordinary circumstances procedures when deciding whether to use this streamlined process.

sections.title: Section 204  Intervenor Status
sections.text: This section gives local governments and Indian tribes special rights in court cases about certain forest projects. If a project is on federal land near their jurisdiction, has been approved by the government, and either reduces wildfire or disease risks or generates timber revenue, then local governments and tribes have the right to join any lawsuit about the project. They also get to participate fully in any settlement negotiations if they join the case. The government agency decides whether the federal land has sufficient connections to the local government or tribal land.

sections.title: Section 205  Utilizing Grazing for Wildfire Risk Reduction
sections.text: Within eighteen months of this law passing, the agencies must develop and send Congress a strategy for using livestock grazing to reduce wildfire risks on federal land. The strategy must analyze opportunities to increase use of grazing authorities, modify grazing permits to allow changes, use targeted grazing to reduce dangerous fuels, use advanced technologies to better control where livestock graze, use grazing to fight invasive grasses and help areas recover after fires, and make it easier to temporarily use empty grazing areas during extreme weather or disasters. The strategy goes to the Senate Committee on Energy and Natural Resources and House Committee on Natural Resources. This section does not affect any existing grazing programs or legal authorities.

sections.title: Section 301  Mandatory Use of Existing Authorities
sections.text: Within three years of this law passing, for each unit of federal land that contains areas with high insect and disease risks or high wildfire danger, the agencies must use at least one streamlined environmental review process. The section lists six specific legal authorities from the Healthy Forests Restoration Act, the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, and the Wildfire Suppression Funding and Forest Management Activities Act that agencies can choose from to speed up environmental reviews.

sections.title: Section 302  Public-Private Wildfire Technology Deployment and Testbed Partnership
sections.text: This section creates a seven-year pilot program to test new wildfire technologies. The Secretaries of Agriculture and Interior must establish the program within sixty days, working with eight types of federal agencies involved in wildfire response including land management agencies, Department of Defense, weather agencies, and emergency management. The program will work with private companies, nonprofits, and colleges to test new technologies. The agencies will identify priority technology areas like hazardous fuels reduction, emergency communications, remote sensing, safety equipment, and operational dashboards. Companies and organizations can apply to test their technologies, with priority given to those developing emerging technologies like artificial intelligence, quantum sensing, augmented reality, and advanced communications networks. The agencies must publicize the priority areas and invite applications. Each year, they must report to four Congressional committees about participating organizations, technologies tested, costs, outreach efforts, and recommendations for adopting new technologies at larger scales. The program ends seven years after this law passes.

sections.title: Section 303  Repeal of FLAME Reports
sections.text: This section eliminates a reporting requirement from the FLAME Act of two thousand nine. It removes the requirement for agencies to submit annual reports about their FLAME (Federal Land Assistance Management and Enhancement) accounts and renumbers the remaining subsections in that law.
